topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something heavy dropping onto a hard surface. My fingers, which had been hovering over a half-finished crossword puzzle, froze mid-air. I clicked it open, the subject line—'Party Update'—a cruel joke in the context of the cheerful anticipation I’d been nursing for weeks. The words swam before my eyes, blurring the bright, imagined scene of confetti and laughter. A slow, cold dread began to coil in my stomach, tightening like a physical band. I reread the message. The cancellation. My breath hitched, a small, sharp intake of air that seemed to steal all the oxygen from the room. I leaned back in my chair, the plastic groaning in protest beneath my weight. My gaze drifted to the framed photo on the desk—me, beaming, surrounded by smiling faces—and a strange hollowness bloomed in my chest. I didn't move to delete the message. Instead, I just sat there, letting the silence of the room amplify the sudden, vast emptiness where joy had been scheduled to reside.

[story 2]

When Sarah told me, the words seemed to arrive in slow motion, each syllable weighted with disbelief. I stared at her, my mouth slightly agape, the color draining from my face until I felt strangely pale. For a long moment, I couldn't process the information. My hands started to tremble, and I had to press them flat against the cool wood of the table to steady them. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a profound disorientation. The entire trajectory of my week—the secret planning, the careful coordination of gifts, the sheer, vibrant hope I had invested—collapsed into a single, unexpected flatline. I looked around the room, at the decorations still half-hung, at the empty space where the celebration was supposed to be. A strange, quiet tremor ran through my limbs. I managed a weak, almost breathless sound, a noise that was half-laugh, half-gasp, before I simply sank into the nearest armchair, feeling suddenly too large for my own skin.

[story 3]

I kept refreshing the screen, a nervous tic taking over my movements. Each new notification was a fresh wave of deflation. The realization hit me not as a sudden shock, but as a slow, creeping realization that settled deep in my bones, chilling me to the core. I stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the living room three times before stopping dead center. My jaw was tight, my eyes wide and unfocused as I tried to reconcile the vibrant tapestry of what I had envisioned with the stark, flat reality presented on the screen. A strange, almost dizzying sensation washed over me—like standing too close to a powerful current. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the texture, grounding myself in the physical world as my internal landscape spun wildly. I let out a slow, shaky exhale, the sound thin and ragged, as the sheer scale of the letdown washed over me.
